cards.title: Code Review Beyond Style
cards.teaser: The most important questions in a code review aren't about formatting — they're about whether the code solves the right problem in a defensible way.

runs.text: Linters catch style. Code review is for everything a linter cannot see.
The questions worth asking in every review:

items.text: Is the assumption baked into this implementation actually true?
A function that parses a date string as UTC "because that's what we get from the API" is a production incident waiting for daylight saving to expose it.
What happens at the boundary?
Empty input, max-size input, concurrent calls, a failure halfway through a multi-step operation.
Is this solving the stated problem or a different one?
Engineers routinely generalize prematurely — building a plugin system when the issue asked for one additional formatter.
Does the error handling reflect how this will actually fail?
Happy-path code with a single broad
catch
is not defensive; it's optimistic fiction.
Would removing this still make the tests pass?
If yes, the tests aren't testing the behavior being added.

runs.text: Spending more review time on naming and formatting than on correctness and assumptions is the most common code review failure mode. Style is recoverable; wrong assumptions ship to production.
The goal is not to block the PR. It is to ask one good question that the author hasn't asked yet. One sharp observation per review does more for long-term code quality than ten nit comments.
Like a structural engineer reviewing blueprints — not checking if the lines are straight, but asking whether the load-bearing walls are in the right places.

cards.title: The "Does This Actually Solve the Problem?" Question in Design Review
cards.teaser: Design reviews routinely evaluate the proposed solution without first confirming that the problem statement is accurate. That's the wrong order.

runs.text: The most expensive design review failure is approving an elegant solution to the wrong problem. It happens because the review context anchors everyone on the proposed design, not on the underlying need.
A disciplined design review runs two passes:
Pass 1: Interrogate the problem statement.

items.text: Is the problem scoped correctly, or has it been inflated to justify a larger solution?
What evidence establishes that this is the actual root cause, not a symptom?
Who is affected, and at what frequency? A problem that hits one user once a quarter warrants a different investment than one that hits every user on every request.
What would happen if we did nothing?

runs.text: Pass 2: Evaluate the proposed solution against the problem.

items.text: Does this design address the problem as stated in Pass 1?
What assumptions does the design rely on? Which are validated?
What failure modes does it introduce? Not just what can go wrong — what will go wrong when the system is under load or when an assumption changes?
Is the complexity proportional to the problem?

runs.text: Before the design review meeting, circulate the problem statement separately from the solution. Ask attendees to evaluate the problem framing before they see the proposed approach. This breaks the anchoring effect.
A design that survives both passes is ready to build. A design that skips Pass 1 is fast to approve and slow to ship.
Like a doctor who asks "what's actually hurting and why" before deciding whether surgery is warranted — not because surgery is bad, but because diagnosis comes before treatment.

cards.title: Staying Analytical During Incident Response
cards.teaser: The first plausible explanation for an incident is almost always incomplete. The pressure to act fast is highest exactly when the cost of acting on a wrong hypothesis is highest.

runs.text: Incident response creates conditions that are hostile to good reasoning: time pressure, multiple voices, partial information, and high stakes. These conditions produce a specific failure: the team latches onto the first explanation that sounds plausible and stops investigating.
The symptoms of premature hypothesis lock-in:

items.text: Rollback initiated before evidence confirms the deploy caused the issue.
Metrics being read selectively — noticing the ones that confirm the hypothesis, skipping the ones that don't.
"We've seen this before" used as a substitute for actually confirming the current incident matches the prior one.
Fixes applied in parallel without isolation — making it impossible to know what actually resolved it.

runs.text: Practices that keep analysis honest under pressure:

items.text: Write the hypothesis down.
"We think X is causing Y because Z." The act of writing forces precision and makes it visible when evidence doesn't match.
Name what would falsify it.
If the hypothesis is correct, what should be true that you can check in the next two minutes?
Assign one person to devil's advocate.
Their job is to actively look for evidence against the working theory.
Isolate before fixing.
If you apply three changes at once, you learn nothing about root cause and have set up the next identical incident.

runs.text: The goal during an active incident is not to find the one true cause — it is to restore service while preserving enough information to find the true cause in the postmortem. Speed and accuracy have different owners during the incident.
Like debugging with print statements — you need to isolate the variable before you can trust the output, or you're just adding noise.

cards.title: The Premature-Blame Trap in Postmortems
cards.teaser: Postmortems that assign personal fault produce two outcomes: defensive engineers and identical future incidents.

runs.text: A postmortem that concludes "Alice deployed without checking the runbook" has learned nothing useful. It cannot prevent the next incident, because it has misidentified the mechanism.
The correct question is not "who did the wrong thing" but "what conditions made the wrong thing easy to do and the right thing hard?"
The five whys applied to personal fault is not five whys — it's one why followed by a name:

items.text: Why did the service go down? → Alice's deploy.
Why did Alice deploy without checking? → She didn't follow the process.

runs.text: The same five whys applied to systems:

items.text: Why did the service go down? → The deploy didn't have a canary stage.
Why not? → The deployment pipeline doesn't enforce it.
Why not? → Canary was added as optional two years ago and never made mandatory.
Why not? → The oncall rotation rotates every quarter and nobody owned it.
Why not? → We have no ownership model for pipeline configuration.

runs.text: The systemic version is harder to write but produces actionable items: make canary mandatory, add an ownership model. The blame version produces: "remind engineers to read the runbook" — an action item that addresses nothing structural.

If every action item from a postmortem is a training or reminder, the postmortem was blame-framed. Structural failures produce structural fixes; training fixes produce the same incident in six months.
Like fixing a factory defect by telling workers to be more careful — it feels like action but it doesn't change the process that produced the defect.

cards.title: Critical Thinking in Sprint Planning
cards.teaser: Sprint planning is where overconfidence, scope inflation, and "while we're at it" thinking compound into commitments that can't be met.

runs.text: Sprint planning has a consistent set of reasoning failures. Naming them makes them easier to catch in the room.
Anchoring on best-case estimates.
Estimates given without explicit consideration of interrupt work, code review cycles, unknowns, and vacation produce sprints that are 60% achievable on a good week.
Scope inflation.
A task that starts as "fix the login timeout" becomes "fix the login timeout and refactor the auth module while we're in there." The original estimate is kept. The scope is not.
"While we're at it" additions.

Related to scope inflation but subtler — a second engineer suggests adding a feature adjacent to the current work because "we'll be touching that code anyway." The cost gets bundled into the existing estimate rather than evaluated independently.
Complexity compression.

"It's just a config change" covers a wide range of actual work. Tasks that feel simple in description are often complex in execution because they depend on systems nobody fully understands. The correct response to "it's just X" is to ask what the last three "just X" tasks actually took.
Questions that make sprint planning more honest:

items.text: What's the actual last task we estimated like this? How long did it take?
What's not in this estimate? (Meetings, review cycles, deploy time, documentation.)
If we add this scope, what do we remove?

runs.text: Treat "while we're at it" as a trigger phrase. It is almost always introducing unestimated work. Either scope it separately or defer it to the next sprint.
Like construction estimates — the scope creep isn't malicious, it's just that each individual addition seems small until you're six months over schedule and haven't started the original work.

cards.title: Structured Decision Frameworks — When They Help and When They Don't
cards.teaser: Decision matrices and DACI charts are tools for reducing noise in high-stakes decisions — they are not a substitute for actually understanding the problem.

runs.text: Structured frameworks for engineering decisions exist because high-stakes choices made in a meeting room are vulnerable to whoever talks loudest, the most senior person's preference, and recency bias toward whichever option was discussed last.
DACI (Driver, Approver, Contributor, Informed)

is useful when a decision cuts across multiple teams and it's unclear who owns the outcome. Assigning roles before the discussion removes the ambiguity about who can veto and who is advisory — which matters when teams disagree.
Decision matrices
(score options against weighted criteria) are useful when:

items.text: There are more than two options being compared.
The criteria for a good decision are contested.
The team needs to make the weighting of criteria explicit rather than implicit.

runs.text: They add friction and reduce value when:

items.text: The right answer is obvious and the matrix is being used to justify a decision already made.
The criteria are not agreed on before scoring (post-hoc weighting makes any option win).
The decision is reversible and low-stakes — the overhead of the framework exceeds the cost of a wrong choice.

runs.text: The most common misuse: treating a completed matrix as a proof rather than a tool. A decision matrix shows what you decided to optimize for and which option scored best against those weights. It doesn't prove the option is correct — it proves it's consistent with your stated priorities.

The value of a decision framework is mostly in forcing the team to articulate criteria before evaluating options. That articulation surfaces disagreements early. A matrix that produces consensus on criteria but divergence on scoring is doing its job — it's found where the real disagreement is.

Like a structured code review checklist — not a replacement for thinking, but a way to ensure the same questions get asked every time and that important ones don't get skipped because they were uncomfortable.

cards.title: Critical Thinking in Hiring
cards.teaser: Unstructured interviews reliably produce hires that reflect the interviewers' biases — not the candidate's actual ability to do the job.

runs.text: Hiring decisions made on gut feel are reproducible only in the sense that they reproduce the same biases every time. The two most common distortions:
Halo effect.

One strong signal (impressive company on resume, confident delivery, fast answer to one question) casts a positive glow over the entire evaluation. Subsequent answers are unconsciously interpreted charitably.
Horn effect.
One weak signal (nervous under pressure, unfamiliar with one specific tool, stumbled on a question) casts a negative glow. Strong subsequent answers are interpreted as outliers.
Structural practices that reduce both:

items.text: Write the evaluation criteria before the interview.
What does a strong answer to this question look like? What does a weak one look like? Agree before you see the candidate.
Use the same questions for every candidate in the same role.
This makes comparisons valid — you're scoring the same performance, not different performances.
Score independently before discussing.
Interviewers who discuss before scoring will converge toward the opinion of the most senior person in the room.
Ask for evidence, not claims.

"Tell me about a time you had to change your technical approach based on new information" produces verifiable specifics. "Are you good at handling feedback?" produces whatever the candidate thinks you want to hear.

runs.text: "Culture fit" as an evaluation criterion without a written definition of what fit means is a halo-effect label. Define what behaviors constitute good collaboration before the interview, or remove the criterion.
Like a blind taste test versus a branded one — the same wine scores differently depending on whether tasters know the label, which tells you the label was doing work the wine should be doing.

cards.title: The "Let's Revisit in Two Weeks" Habit
cards.teaser: Decisions made under uncertainty don't improve by being revisited — they improve by being revisited with new information. The discipline is in scheduling the revisit and actually running it.

runs.text: A high fraction of engineering decisions are made before all the relevant information exists: before the feature has real users, before the new service has production traffic, before the refactor has run through a full release cycle. Acknowledging this and building in a deliberate revisit is not indecision — it is accurate calibration.
What makes a revisit effective versus performative:
Set the revisit conditions at decision time.

"We'll revisit this in two weeks" is not useful without "and at that point we will check X and Y to evaluate whether the assumption holds." Without named checkpoints, the revisit becomes a status update rather than a re-evaluation.
Assign an owner for the revisit.
Unowned revisits do not happen. The decision closes, the next sprint starts, and the assumption goes unexamined until it surfaces as an incident.
Define in advance what outcome would change the decision.
If error rates above 2% would prompt a rollback, write that down at decision time. Defining success and failure criteria before seeing the results prevents post-hoc rationalization of bad outcomes.
Run the revisit even when things look fine.

"It's working" is not the same as "our assumption was correct." Sometimes a decision works for reasons unrelated to the assumption it was built on. Understanding why it worked matters for the next decision.

The broader habit this builds: treating decisions as hypotheses that need validation rather than conclusions that need defense. Teams with this habit accumulate calibrated confidence over time. Teams without it repeat the same mistakes in different sprints.
Add the revisit as a calendar event or backlog ticket at decision time, not as a mental note. Mental notes do not survive sprint handoffs.
Like a feature flag with a defined removal date — the decision to ship the flag is only complete when the conditions for flipping it permanently or rolling it back are written down.
